title: Anthropic ships a self-hosted Claude apps gateway for Bedrock and Google Cloud
subtitle: A stateless container with corporate SSO, spend caps, and per-user attribution — designed to end the credential sprawl of deploying Claude Code at enterprise scale.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Anthropic released the Claude apps gateway, a self-hosted control plane for running Claude Code against Amazon Bedrock, Google Cloud, or the Claude API from a single deployment. The gateway is a stateless container backed by PostgreSQL. It holds an organization's upstream credential, authenticates developers against the corporate identity provider, distributes managed settings, and reports per-user usage to a customer-operated collector. Routing supports failover between providers, and admins can set daily, weekly, and monthly spend caps at organization, group, or user granularity.

The problem this solves is unglamorous but real. Enterprise Claude Code rollouts typically require each developer to hold their own upstream API keys or cloud credentials, which turns into a governance headache: shadow spend, no attribution, and a fresh credential rotation whenever an employee leaves. The gateway centralizes the credential and decentralizes only the usage telemetry — with an explicit guarantee that inference traffic does not go to Anthropic unless the gateway is configured to hit the Claude API directly.

Notably, this is being shipped by Anthropic itself rather than by the cloud partners. That reflects where the friction actually lives: enterprises adopting AI coding tools don't want to relitigate identity and cost governance for every model provider. A single gateway that fronts three back-ends is a bet that customers will accept a small amount of Anthropic-controlled tooling in exchange for consolidating governance across the two hyperscalers they were already using.

For learners in platform or DevOps roles: identity, spend caps, and per-user attribution are becoming the boring-but-load-bearing layer of enterprise AI adoption. The interesting technical problems in enterprise AI over the next year are less about model choice and more about the plumbing — SSO, quota enforcement, credential rotation, audit trails, and provider failover. That's a career surface every enterprise will need staffed, and almost none have staffed it yet.
